disease.name: Colonic Polyps (GI Bleeding)
disease.note: Colonic polyps causing painless rectal bleeding. Most common cause of painless hematochezia in children is juvenile polyp (hamartomatous, benign, self-limiting, auto-amputates). In adults, adenomatous polyps bleed indicating precancerous lesion requiring polypectomy. Types: juvenile, adenomatous (tubular/villous/tubulovillous), hyperplastic, sessile serrated.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Massive bleed/intussusception -> URGENT; most -> elective colonoscopy and polypectomy

subtypes.name: Juvenile Polyp (Hamartomatous)
subtypes.notes: Children age 2-10, pedunculated, rectum/sigmoid, benign, auto-amputates

subtypes.name: Tubular Adenoma
subtypes.notes: Most common adenoma, low malignant potential, pedunculated

subtypes.name: Villous Adenoma
subtypes.notes: High malignant potential, sessile, often in rectum

subtypes.name: Sessile Serrated Lesion
subtypes.notes: Right colon, BRAF mutated, subtle endoscopic appearance

secondLine.test: Flexible sigmoidoscopy (if polyp in reachable area)
Genetic testing (if multiple polyps or family history)

investigations.goldStandard: Colonoscopy with polypectomy and histopathological examination

emergency: Urgent polypectomy if actively bleeding from polyp stalk
outpatient: Endoscopic polypectomy (snare for pedunculated, EMR/ESD for sessile)
Surveillance colonoscopy per guidelines (3 years for high-risk adenoma, 5-10 for low-risk)
Juvenile polyp: polypectomy curative, repeat colonoscopy in 1 year if multiple
Colon cancer screening initiation at age 45 (average risk) or earlier with family history
refractory: Surgical resection if endoscopic polypectomy not feasible (large sessile, malignant)
preventive: Colon cancer screening starting at age 45 for average risk
Adenoma surveillance colonoscopy at 3 years for high-risk, 5-10 for low-risk
Lifestyle: maintain healthy weight, exercise, limit red meat, avoid smoking

disease.pathophysiology: Juvenile polyps: developmental abnormality of lamina propria → dilated cystic glands → surface erosion → painless bleeding. Adenomatous polyps: APC mutation → deregulated Wnt/β-catenin pathway → crypt dysplasia → adenoma → surface friability → bleeding. Hamartomatous polyps: Peutz-Jeghers - STK11 mutation, FAP - APC germline mutation.

complications.complication: Iron deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss

mustNotMiss: Juvenile polyp is benign but multiple juvenile polyps or family history suggests juvenile polyposis syndrome (SMAD4/BMPR1A mutation)
Adenoma-carcinoma sequence: remove ALL adenomatous polyps, surveillance is essential
Sessile serrated lesions are easily missed on colonoscopy - look for mucus cap, indistinct borders
Melanosis coli from chronic anthraquinone laxative use can mimic polyp pigmentation - benign

diagnosticClues.clue: Painless bright red blood per rectum in child age 2-10 - suspect juvenile polyp
Colonoscopy shows pedunculated erythematous polyp in rectum/sigmoid (juvenile)
Adult >45 with intermittent rectal bleeding + iron deficiency anemia - suspect adenomatous polyp
Colonoscopy shows pedunculated or sessile polyp with characteristic pit pattern

exclusionClues.clue: Pain with defecation + blood streak (anal fissure)
More massive bleeding with currant-jelly stool in child (Meckel)
